questions.prompt: Which of these does Google explicitly say is NOT a ranking factor?

choices.text: The number of high-quality external sites that link to your page.
Whether your content is helpful to users.

questions.explanation: Google has said explicitly for over a decade: 'Google Search doesn't use the keywords meta tag.' Backlinks (a), helpfulness (b), and page experience (d) are all real signals. Meta keywords does literally nothing.

questions.prompt: Your SEO consultant says your articles must hit 2,500 words to rank. What's the most accurate assessment?

choices.text: They're right — Google's algorithm has a hard word-count floor.

Wrong. Google explicitly says there's no magical word count target. Length should be determined by what the topic actually needs. Padding articles to hit arbitrary counts makes content worse, not better.
They're right for YMYL but wrong for other topics.
They're right but only for AI Overviews.

questions.explanation: Google's SEO Starter Guide is direct: 'The length of the content alone doesn't matter for ranking purposes (there's no magical word count target, minimum or maximum, though you probably want to have at least one word).' Thorough content tends to rank well — but the cause is thoroughness, not word count. Padding hurts.
